claim: WHO estimates that as many as 18 million children and adolescents and 12.9 million women may be at risk from adverse health outcomes linked to e-waste recycling.

source.title: Children and digital dumpsites: e-waste exposure and child health

method: Reviewed the WHO report summary, burden estimates, exposure-pathway discussion, vulnerable-population section, and stated research priorities.
limitations: The estimate describes people potentially at risk, not confirmed exposed individuals or attributable disease cases.
The global estimate does not identify city-level clusters or establish an intervention threshold.

claim: The Global E-waste Monitor reports 62 billion kg of e-waste generated globally in 2022 and 22.3% documented as formally collected and recycled in an environmentally sound manner.
method: Reviewed the ITU publication page and report headline indicators for generation, documented formal collection and recycling, regional coverage, and projection methodology.
limitations: Documented formal collection is not the same as total safe processing and does not directly measure informal exposure.
National and regional accounting cannot locate individual processing clusters or prove where undocumented material is processed.

claim: WHO identifies children and pregnant women as particularly vulnerable to hazardous substances released through unsound e-waste recycling and describes evidence across respiratory, neurological, developmental, thyroid, and pregnancy-related outcomes.
method: Reviewed WHO sections on exposure pathways, vulnerable groups, health effects, and recommended health-sector actions.
limitations: The fact sheet synthesizes evidence and does not provide a local exposure-response function.
The listed outcomes cannot be attributed to e-waste at an individual or neighborhood level without primary study design and measurement.

claim: A peer-reviewed Ghana study measured metal exposure and estimated human-health risks in and around the Agbogbloshie informal e-waste processing site, demonstrating the need to separate site-specific measurements from global proxy mapping.

source.title: Human health risk associated with metal exposure at Agbogbloshie e-waste site and the surrounding neighbourhood in Accra, Ghana

method: Reviewed the study design, sampled media and locations, analytes, exposure-assessment assumptions, risk calculations, and limitations in the full text.
limitations: A single Ghana site cannot establish a transferable global threshold or represent all e-waste processing methods.
Modeled risk estimates depend on exposure assumptions and do not identify which intervention would reduce exposure.

claim: The ILO describes e-waste work as a heterogeneous value chain in which low- and middle-income countries rely more heavily on informal, labour-intensive collection, repair, refurbishment, recycling, and material recovery.

source.title: E-waste and employment around the world

method: Reviewed the ILO publication description and sections describing the e-waste value chain, formal and informal work, and measurement needs for employment and occupational protection.
limitations: Employment descriptions do not quantify toxic exposure or prove that formalization improves health.
Global value-chain framing cannot substitute for local worker, livelihood, or processing-activity data.
